title: Limits of Hand-Crafted Features
concept: Hand-designing a filter pipeline for one object or scenario doesn't scale to the real world — new objects need new hand-tuned filters, and even a working pipeline breaks under lighting, angle, or occlusion changes it wasn't built for.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In a classical CV pipeline, who decides the exact filters and the order they run in?

options.text: The image decides on its own
It's randomly generated on every run

exercises.explain: Right — a human designs and orders every filter. Today we look at why that hand-tuning stops scaling.

cards.title: One pipeline, a thousand exceptions
cards.body: Say you hand-tune a pipeline that spots stop signs perfectly... in daylight. Now it's dusk, the sign is half-covered by a tree branch, and the camera is tilted 15 degrees. Suddenly your carefully tuned filters miss it entirely.

cards.title: The limits of hand-crafted features
cards.body: Hand-crafted features are filters and thresholds a human designs and tunes for one specific task. The core limit: they don't generalize well — a pipeline tuned for stop signs in daylight won't reliably work on cats, or on stop signs at night, without a person redesigning it all over again.

cards.title: A key cut for one lock
cards.body: A hand-crafted filter pipeline is like a key cut for exactly one lock. It works perfectly on that lock — but a new lock, meaning a new object, new lighting, or a new angle, needs a brand new key cut by hand. That doesn't scale to millions of different locks.

cards.title: Same object, different day
cards.body: A Sobel-based pipeline tuned to trace a car's edges on a bright day may fail on an overcast day, where gradients are weaker. It may fail again when the car is half-hidden behind a pole, or when the camera angle changes and the edge shapes look completely different.

cards.title: More filters isn't the fix
cards.body: It's tempting to think 'just add more filters' solves this. But every new lighting condition, angle, or object needs its own hand-tuned rule — the list of special cases grows faster than any engineer could ever maintain by hand.

cards.title: One task vs. the real world
cards.body: A tight fit vs. endless variety:

left.text:
Works great
on the exact case it was built for

exercises.prompt: Why don't hand-crafted filter pipelines scale to recognizing thousands of different objects?

options.text: Each new object or condition typically needs a human to redesign and re-tune the filters by hand
Filters run too slowly to ever process more than one object
Filters only work on grayscale images
Filters can only be applied to square images

exercises.explain: The bottleneck is human effort — someone has to hand-design new filters for every new object or condition.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A pipeline tuned to detect edges in bright daylight will automatically work just as well at night.
exercises.explain: False. Weaker light means weaker gradients, and a pipeline tuned for daylight thresholds often misses edges entirely at night.

exercises.sentence: A pipeline hand-tuned for one specific object or lighting condition often fails to ___ to new, unseen conditions.
exercises.explain: Generalize is correct — hand-crafted filters are brittle outside the exact case they were tuned for.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario best illustrates the brittleness of a hand-crafted pipeline?

options.text: A Sobel-based sign detector that worked perfectly in testing fails once the sign is partly blocked by a tree branch
A photo loads a fraction of a second slower on an older phone
A JPEG file takes up more storage than a same-size PNG

exercises.explain: Occlusion is a classic brittleness case — the hand-tuned filter never accounted for a partially hidden object.

exercises.prompt: An engineer spends 6 months hand-tuning a pipeline to detect pedestrians in daylight. Their boss then asks them to also detect pedestrians at night, in the rain, and from a different camera angle. What's the real problem here?

options.text: Every new condition likely needs its own set of hand-tuned filters and thresholds — hand-crafting doesn't scale to real-world variety
There's no real problem — the exact same daylight pipeline will work unchanged for every condition
The engineer just needs to increase the image resolution and everything will work
Rain and night driving are actually easier for a hand-crafted pipeline than daylight

exercises.explain: Exactly — six more months of hand-tuning for each new condition isn't sustainable. What if, instead of a human tuning every filter, the filters could be learned automatically from examples? That's exactly where we're headed next.
